perturbation.original_text: Aura shall remit payment to Zanotti for the invoiced price of any given Product within the earlier of: (i) fifteen (15) business days following Aura's receipt and acceptance of payment from its customer for such AETRU System into which such Product is incorporated; or (ii) forty-five (45) calendar days from the date of Aura's shipment to a customer of such AETRU System into which such Product is incorporated.
perturbation.changed_text: Aura shall remit payment to Zanotti for the invoiced price of any given Product within the earlier of: (i) ninety (90) business days following Aura's receipt and acceptance of payment from its customer for such AETRU System into which such Product is incorporated; or (ii) one hundred and eighty (180) calendar days from the date of Aura's shipment to a customer of such AETRU System into which such Product is incorporated.
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms beyond what is commercially reasonable or customary can create issues under California's Prompt Payment laws, especially if Zanotti is a small business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5 mandates that home improvement contracts cannot require the owner to pay the contractor an unreasonable amount of overhead and profit. Extended payment terms such as 90-180 days could be deemed unreasonable, especially if Zanotti is a smaller supplier dependent on timely payments.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms significantly (from 15-45 days to 90-180 days). The Law Explanation suggests this could be deemed unreasonable under California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5, especially for smaller suppliers. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, concerns home improvement contracts and states that failure to comply with its provisions is cause for discipline. While the snippet doesn't directly address payment timelines, the Law Explanation reasonably infers that excessively extended payment terms could violate the requirement for fair dealing, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by highlighting potential issues under California's Prompt Payment laws, especially if Zanotti is a small business.

perturbation.original_text: Unless terminated prior to the natural expiration of the Initial Term, upon the expiry of the Initial Term this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive terms of the same duration, unless either party gives written notice to the other of such party's desire not to renew not less than ninety (90) days prior to the date of the expiration of the Initial Term or any successive term thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless terminated prior to the natural expiration of the Initial Term, upon the expiry of the Initial Term this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive terms of the same duration, unless either party gives written notice to the other of such party's desire not to renew not less than twenty (20) days prior to the date of the expiration of the Initial Term or any successive term thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period to 20 days creates potential non-compliance with California franchise law, especially if the agreement is interpreted as a franchise.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Franchise Relations Act, Business and Professions Code Section 20000 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Franchise Relations Act requires a 180-day notice period for non-renewal of a franchise agreement. If this agreement is interpreted as a franchise, the 20-day notice period is insufficient and violates California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 20000 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 8. SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 1. Definitions [20000 - 20010] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20000. This chapter shall be known and may be referred to as the California Franchise Relations Act. (Added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. Effective October 1, 1980. Operative January 1, 1981, by Sec. 6 of Ch. 1355.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-renewal notice period from 90 days to 20 days. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this creates potential non-compliance with California franchise law. The Law Explanation states California's Franchise Relations Act requires a 180-day notice period for non-renewal of a franchise agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 20000, confirms the existence of the California Franchise Relations Act. Therefore, the modified text directly contradicts the requirement for a 180-day notice period, if the agreement is deemed a franchise agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations with respect to all other Confidential Information shall continue for a period of five (5) years following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations with respect to all other Confidential Information shall continue for a period of ten (10) years following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 10 years for non-trade secret information could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A ten-year confidentiality period, particularly if it covers information that does not qualify as a trade secret, could be considered an unlawful restraint of trade, making it unenforceable in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period to 10 years, which may violate California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as it could be considered an unlawful restraint of trade. The original text has a confidentiality period of 5 years, and the change increases that. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A longer confidentiality period increases the restraint, potentially making it an illegal restraint of trade under California law, and creating a legal contradiction.
